data.title: Notable tech headlines for the week: Apple, Oracle, Intel in focus
data.description:
The tech sector remained in the spotlight this week as investors focused on the blockbuster IPO from SpaceX, more earnings headlines as well as a revamped AI strategy from Apple.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed 0.3% higher on Friday.

For the week, the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) fell 0.5% during the week. The top S&P 500 technology gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

KLA Corp (KLAC) +31.94%

Sandisk (SNDK) +26.92%

Intel (INTC) +25.58%

Applied Materials (AMAT) +25.18%

Lam Research (LRCX) +20.95%

Top Losers:

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) -26.85%

Adobe (ADBE) -18.86%

PTC (PTC) -17.02%

Oracle (ORCL) -13.83%

Autodesk (ADSK) -13.71%

Here are some of the important technology stories from this week:

Apple launched long-awaited refresh to Siri 

Apple (AAPL) announced at its annual Worldwide Developers Conference that it is bringing Siri to the artificial intelligence chatbot age.

Mike Rockwell, Apple's Siri engineering vice president, said it is more capable and more conversational and has a “brand new design that reflects the power of Apple Intelligence.” The tech giant has already begun the process of monetizing it.

Wedbush believes the AI monetization and services will ultimately add $75 to $100 to Apple's stock and that is not being factored into the current multiple.

"WWDC 2026 illustrated clear progress on Apple's AI roadmap and suggested earlier monetization oppt'y than we expected," said Morgan Stanley analyst Erik Woodring.

Intel gets double upgrade from BofA

Intel (INTC) grabbed limelight this week after BofA double upgraded the chipmaker's stock to Buy from Underperform and raised PT to $135 from $96.

The brokerage said it has a higher confidence in Intel's opportunity to help address industry constraints in leading edge wafers/packaging, plus supply into a much larger agentic CPU Total Addressable Market, or TAM.

On the other hand, research firm SemiAnalysis said the Lip-Bu Tan-led company should consider raising additional capital to help with its turnaround and expansion efforts.

According to a report from The Information, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA) are considering Intel as a backup chip manufacturer. Alphabet's unit Google has reportedly ordered more than 3M Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs, from Intel. Nvidia has not yet placed an order with Intel. The company is testing if Intel technology can be used to make a forthcoming processor, the report added.

Oracle, Adobe in focus on earnings news

Oracle (ORCL) closed over 8% lower on Thursday after Q4 results, as analysts highlighted investor concerns over gross margin pressure and capital raise plans.

Oracle forecast higher FY27capex than analysts' estimates and said it expects to raise about $40B in debt and equity in fiscal 2027 to support its capital investment program.

Similarly, Adobe's (ADBE) second quarter results once again topped consensus estimates, but its CFO transition at the top combined with its continued freemium push on AI-enabled products create some uncertainties, prompting multiple downgrades from Wall Street analysts.

Other important news that was in focus included U.S. Department of Defense including tech giants Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) within a list of Chinese military companies operating in the U.S.

Supermicro (SMCI) revealed a series of equity and equity-linked financing transactions totaling $7B to help it fund the purchase of components necessary to complete recent orders for its advanced AI servers.

In its historic IPO, SpaceX (SPCX) officially opened on the Nasdaq at $150, and ended the session on Friday with a gain of nearly 20%. The offering made Elon Musk, who leads both SpaceX and Tesla (TSLA), the world's first trillionaire.

Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said the recent selloff in U.S. semiconductor stocks should be viewed in the context of a much larger trend, stating that "We are at the outset of the AI revolution."

"We're at the beginning of it, and whatever happened to the stock market, you should be very happy because now you can buy at a discount. Everybody should be very excited." Huang told reporters in Seoul.

data.title: J&J multiple myeloma drug Talvey cuts mortality risk by up to 53% in late-stage trial
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced on Saturday that its bispecific T-cell engager Talvey, when used with Halozyme (HALO)-partnered Darzalex Faspro, reduced the risk of death by up to 53% compared to standard treatment in a late-stage trial for multiple myeloma.

Talvey, a GPRC5D-targeting bispecific antibody therapy, is already approved in the U.S. for the treatment of those with the blood cancer who have undergone at least four prior lines of therapy. Darzalex Faspro is a front-line multiple myeloma therapy.

The MonumenTAL-3 trial is designed to evaluate Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro with or without FDA-approved MM therapy pomalidomide versus Darzalex Faspro plus pomalidomide and dexamethasone (DPd) in patients with RRMM who have received at least a single line of therapy.

Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro, compared to the standard-of-care regimen, cut the risk of cancer progression or death and overall risk of death by up to 72% and 53%, respectively, J&J (JNJ) said, citing a median follow-up of roughly two years.

Additionally, at 24 months, about 89% of patients who received Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro were alive (overall survival) compared to 79% of those on standard of care, while 81% indicated progression-free survival ((PFS)) compared to 51% on standard of care.

The safety profiles of investigational arms were consistent with those of individual drugs, the company said, adding that MonumenTAL-3 is the first late-stage trial to demonstrate PFS superiority for a GPRC5D bispecific antibody combo for MM in an earlier-line setting.

J&J (JNJ) has already sought label expansions for Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro in the U.S. and EU, targeting RRMM patients who have previously received at least one line of therapy.

data.title: Anthropic takes latest AI models offline after government directive
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) is disabling access to its artificial intelligence models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for all customers after the Trump administration issued a directive to prevent foreign nationals from accessing the latest AI systems on national security grounds.

The export control directive received at 5:21 pm ET applied to any foreign national, including its employees with an overseas nationality, the Amazon (AMZN)-backed AI startup said, adding that access to its other AI models will not be affected.

“The US government, citing national security authorities, has issued an export control directive to suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees,” the company said.

“The net effect of this order is that we must abruptly disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all our customers to ensure compliance.”

data.title: KFC vibes: Yum! Brands tests a new concept for Colonel Sanders
data.description:
Yum! Brands (YUM) is looking to invigorate the KFC brand by testing a new Open House concept in McKinney, Texas. The KFC location will be positioned as a more contemporary, hospitality‑driven spin on its traditional fast-food format.

The restaurant company describes Open House as a next‑generation KFC restaurant, with a modern kitchen, upgraded interior build‑out, and a more welcoming, hang‑out‑friendly environment than legacy units. Located near Yum Brands’ (YUM) Plano headquarters, the first site functions as a live test bed where KFC management can strategize quickly on design, operations, and digital features. The roughly half‑million‑dollar interior conversion appears to be a ground‑up rethink of layout, decor, and guest flow rather than a light remodel.

The KFC Open House concept fits into the Louisville-based company's broader push to refresh the brand for younger and more digitally native consumers while still leaning on core fried chicken as its calling card. As with its "Saucy by KFC" spinoff in Orlando, which tested tech‑forward ordering, entertainment elements, and a simplified tenders‑and‑sauces menu, Open House gives the chain a sandbox to explore different experiential cues and operational models under the main KFC banner. 

Open House may also test localized programming or community‑centric elements consistent with the “house” metaphor—positioning KFC as a neighborhood gathering spot rather than just a quick-service stop. If Open House resonates in McKinney, KFC could either roll pieces of the format into systemwide remodels or spin it into a distinct sub-format for high‑growth suburban nodes.

Systemwide sales were up 7% year over year for the KFC division in Q1, and same-store sales were 2% higher for the KFC division in Q1. Core operating was up 9%.

data.title: Persian Gulf oil flows reaching 7M bbl/day, helped by U.S. escorts, DoE secretary says
data.description:
~7M bbl/day of crude oil and fuel shipments are flowing through the Strait of Hormuz, or roughly half of the volumes stranded at the start of the U.S.-Iran war, Energy Secretary Chris Wright said Friday, although Chevron (CVX) CEO Mike Wirth raised doubts about that figure.

"We have a military effort ⁠that we've not talked a lot about, which started ​more recently to get cargoes out," Wright told the Bloomberg Energy Security Executive Briefing in Houston.

The count is "a rough average of where we are right now, and it's rising," Wright said, adding the number is about half of the 14M bbl/day gap in supply caused by the de facto closing of the strait, while several million bbl/day also are diverted through regional pipelines.

No Iranian crude is ​getting out of the strait, Wright said, adding that he expects to see the free flow of all products through ​the Persian Gulf if a peace deal is reached; if ​no deal is reached, the U.S. military would work to ‌restore ⁠the flow, the secretary said.

The restoration of 7M bbl/day "is a bigger number than I think people had been expecting," and helps "justify" why Brent crude is not trading closer to $150/bbl, Pickering Energy Partners chief investment officer Dan Pickering told the conference after Wright’s remarks.

Oil prices currently below $90/bbl ​indicate that investors ⁠had assumed only 3M-4M bbl/day of oil were flowing through Hormuz, CIBC Private Wealth senior energy ​trader Rebecca Bain said at the ⁠event.

The Financial Times reported this week that more ships are using alternative and risky routes to exit the strait, protected by the U.S. Navy and air cover.

Chevron (CVX) CEO Mike Wirth, who also spoke at the same event, disputed Wright's estimate, saying during a question-and-answer session that "our view would be it's probably not quite that much."

A growing number of reports Friday suggested the U.S. and Iran may sign an interim deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz on the sidelines of the Group of Seven world leaders summit next week, and Wirth also cautioned against making premature assumptions about headlines related to peace negotiations.

"The evidence would say you shouldn't fully believe that until you see the agreement signed and you see the actions begin to come into effect," noting that Iran has a history of being "a patient and immovable negotiating force."

Signs that flows from the Persian Gulf are rising and progress toward an interim deal to end the conflict pushed crude oil futures to multimonth lows Friday.

Iran's foreign minister Araqchi said a memorandum of understanding "has never been closer" but is pending finalization, and Prime Minister Sharif from chief mediator Pakistan said "peace has never been this close."

"Headlines are driving the market once again as confidence grows that an eventual deal will be struck and ​the strait reopens," PVM Oil Associates analyst Tamas Varga said in a note.

On Friday, front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) July delivery slumped 3.2% to $84.88/bbl, its lowest settlement value since April 17, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for August delivery tumbled 3.4% to $87.33/bbl, its weakest closing price since March 5; for the full week, WTI and Brent crude each dropped 6.2%.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for July rose 1.1% on Friday to $3.120/MMBtu; for the week, the front-month contract fell 3.4%.

Energy stocks, as indicated by the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), were little changed on the week.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE).

data.title: Paramount's $110B purchase of Warner Bros. approved by DOJ (update)
data.description:
Update 7:35pm: Adds regulator comment.

The U.S.Justice Justice Department's antitrust division has approved Paramount Skydance 's(PSKY) planned $110 billion purchase of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD).

"The Division has completed its analysis of the proposed merger of Paramount and Warner Bros. and determined based on the evidence received in its investigation that the transaction is not likely to result in harm to competition or American consumers," the DOJ said in a statement on Friday.

The DOJ approval comes after a Reuters report a week ago that several state attorneys plan to file a lawsuit to block  its acquisition by Paramount.

A potential lawsuit by state attorneys general would present another roadblock to regulatory approval, joining the European Union and FCC, who have voiced concerns about the merger. Last Tuesday, UK regulators opened an investigation into the deal, setting an August 7 deadline on whether to initiate a more intensive Phase 3 probe.

News of the DOJ approval was earlier reported by Politico.

Shares of Warner Bros. (WBD) edged higher by 0.5% in after-hours trading, while Paramount Skydance (PSKY) rose 3.3%.

data.title: Clarity Act by July 4 paints “green forest” for Bitcoin—but are bears still patrolling?
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is slowly stepping into a policy-driven “green forest” as expectations around the Clarity Act move closer to final approval.

White House Executive Director Patrick Witt has reportedly pointed to July 4 as the target date for signing the bill. A reported 90-minute closed-door emergency meeting on Friday also focused on roughly $30B in regulatory concerns. 

Senator Tim Scott added that the act will protect consumers and reduce business costs—strengthening the idea that clearer rules could unlock fresh liquidity for crypto markets.

Still, the timeline has shifted multiple times, and that lingering uncertainty continues to weigh on conviction.

On the one-month chart, Bitcoin has rebounded from the recent $59K bottom and is now trading around the $64K range after a sharp multi-week downtrend. The Bitcoin price structure showed an earlier breakdown from the $80K levels, making this bounce more of a relief climb.

The $60K area acted like a strong support floor, absorbing heavy selling, where volume spikes hinted at exhaustion from the bears.

Momentum indicators are improving but still early in recovery. The weekly move shows a ~3% bounce, with RSI climbing from deeply oversold levels near 20 toward the 55–60 zone, while MACD is showing early bullish crossover signals.

However, the path is not fully safe yet. The 24-hour trading volume remains down over 10% at $26.7B, suggesting weak conviction behind the rebound.

Resistance remains firm at $65K, with a stronger supply wall near $68K. Without stronger participation, the bounce risks fading into another range-bound phase.

A green path for Bitcoin builds as the Clarity Act progresses, but whether bears re-enter depends on the $60K support holding strong or breaking down.

data.title: Corteva sees higher restructuring charges, plans to cease production at Spanish site
data.description:
Corteva (CTVA) disclosed Friday it ‌expects higher costs from its multi-year crop protection operations restructuring program ​and plans to cease ​production activities at its site in Asturias, Spain.

The company ​said it now expects to record total pre-tax restructuring and asset-related charges of $750M-$815M, including at least $300M of costs related to exiting the production activities and ceasing operations, compared to prior estimates of $650M-$700M.

Corteva (CTVA) said ​it expects ​cash outflows of $400M-$443M related to the restructuring, primarily ​for decommissioning and demolition, contract terminations, and the payment of severance and related benefits.

Separately, Corteva (CTVA) said Thursday it agreed to pay $85M to resolve farmers' claims in a class action lawsuit accusing it of ​rigging the market for some crop protection products.

The lawsuit alleged Corteva (CTVA) and Syngenta used loyalty programs with distributors to impact competition from generic manufacturers, keeping prices ​of their crop protection products artificially high.

The company denied any wrongdoing ​in agreeing to settle the lawsuit, which was filed ⁠in 2022; the deal requires approval from a judge.

data.title: Inside Lockheed Martin's $2.293B win: F-35 jet long-term sustainment contract
data.description:
• Lockheed Martin won a $2.293B contract for F-35 sustainment support services on Friday.
  • The contract covers F-35 sustainment work, including site activation, fleet management, interim contractor support, and reliability improvements for U.S. Air Force, Navy, Marine, and allied FMS and F-35 program partners.
  • The work is expected to be completed by December 2028, with Naval Air Systems Command managing the contract.
  • No funds are obligated at award; payments will be made as individual orders are issued.
  • On the market side, the stock closed ~1.52% lower at ~$540.33.

data.title: Gold gains on growing U.S.-Iran deal hopes, tempering rate hike expectations
data.description:
Gold futures surged 3% Friday on hopes for a peace deal in the Middle East, as a preliminary deal to end the conflict between the U.S. and Iran that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz appears within reach.

The news bolstered investor risk appetite, helping lift the stock market while pushing crude oil, Treasury yields, and the dollar lower, easing expectations—for now—of rate hikes from the U.S. Federal Reserve.

The U.S., Iran, and chief mediator Pakistan all agreed a deal to end the war was nearly complete, with an initial agreement that would open up a 60-day period during which Iran would open the Strait of Hormuz and the U.S. would wind back its blockade of Iran's ports and commerce.

Negotiations would be held on a final nuclear deal and the sanctions relief Iran would receive under it, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing an unnamed senior U.S. official.

"With oil prices coming down sharply, alongside hopes that the Strait of Hormuz will reopen, that has seen investors price out the chance of rapid rate hikes this year," Deutsche Bank said in a note.

Despite Friday's rebound, gold still finished lower for the week, as a flare-up of attacks between the U.S. and Iran in recent days and U.S. consumer price data led to inflation fears that could ​prompt central banks to keep interest rates higher, raising the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding precious metals.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for June delivery jumped 3% on Friday to $4,215.00/oz, while front-month Comex June silver (XAGUSD:CUR) surged 6.2% to $67.859/oz, snapping five-session losing streaks for both metals.

For the full week, gold and silver fell 2.8% and 1.6%, respectively.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SGDM), (SGDJ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
